does: This paper investigates how multimodal language models process and represent visual information by analyzing the internal key-value tokens that encode image data as they flow through the language model layers. The authors examine three popular MLMs (LLaVA-OneVision, Qwen2.5-VL, and Llama-3-LLaVA-NeXT) and demonstrate that visual value tokens contain sufficient information to perform perception-heavy tasks like segmentation, semantic correspondence, temporal correspondence, and referring expression detection in zero-shot settings. They reveal that while language models augment visual information from visual encoder projections—an augmentation that correlates with overall perception performance—the processed visual tokens often retain less task-relevant visual information than the original unfinetuned visual encoders, suggesting information bottlenecks in MLM architectures.
connects: This work extends the interpretability analysis of transformer-based vision-language models beyond previous VIT encoder and transformer activation studies. It responds to the puzzle of why multimodal language models underperform on perception-heavy tasks despite impressive general capabilities, building on foundational work in mechanistic interpretability of language models and vision transformer analysis. The paper complements concurrent research examining information flow in neural networks and connects to broader efforts understanding how multimodal representations emerge during training, particularly how visual and textual information interact in the projection and processing layers of unified models.
enables: This analysis opens pathways for improving multimodal language model architectures by identifying specific layers and token types where visual information degrades. The findings suggest opportunities to redesign projection mechanisms, preserve visual information more faithfully, or develop auxiliary losses that maintain perceptual capabilities during language model finetuning. The methodology of tracing visual information through MLM layers could generalize to other multimodal architectures and inspire interventions targeting perception-heavy tasks. Understanding these information bottlenecks may also inform better training strategies for balancing language understanding with visual reasoning in future foundation models.
